nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs at 2–30 m depth, most commonly on sheltered reefs among branching corals

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on macroalgae and microalgae; has also been documented consuming small invertebrates in the wild (trophic level 2.7). In captivity, has been known to accept dried seaweed sheets (nori), marine algae, herbivore pellets, and flake foods. Offering a clip of seaweed at least three times a week is generally advised. A well-fed specimen tends to leave corals undisturbed.

summary: A rare, striking rabbitfish endemic to the Andaman Sea, displaying bold black-and-white facial patterning with a bicolored body. Generally peaceful and reef-safe when well fed; has venomous dorsal spines.
careNotes: The Magnificent Foxface is a relatively hardy species generally considered suitable for experienced aquarists (intermediate care). It is endemic to a narrow range in the eastern Indian Ocean — confirmed from the Mergui Archipelago (Myanmar), Surin Islands, and Similan Islands (Thailand), with possible occurrence near the Andaman and Nicobar Islands — making it rarer in the trade than the common Foxface Lo (Siganus vulpinus). Adults are typically observed in pairs in the wild, with juveniles secretive among branching corals. In captivity, this species tends to be kept singly; it has been known to be aggressive toward conspecifics and other rabbitfish, so only one specimen per tank is generally advised. It is generally considered peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates and can tolerate housing with more assertive species. IMPORTANT: All dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous and can inflict a painful sting with swelling and throbbing that may last hours; handle only with a net or aquarium tools, never with bare hands. The Magnificent Foxface has been reported to display color-change camouflage (mottled dark pattern) when stressed, startled, or sleeping — this is a normal behavioral response, not a sign of illness. Reef safety is conditional on feeding: a well-fed specimen is generally considered unlikely to nip at corals, but underfed individuals have been observed nipping at macroalgae, large-polyp stony corals, and soft corals. Provide ample algae and consider daily nori supplementation. Temperature note: aquarium trade sources commonly cite 72–78°F (22–26°C); FishBase habitat models suggest a broader preferred range up to 28°C. The averaged range (22–28°C) is used here; optimal aquarium temperature is generally advised around 24–26°C. Tank size note: LiveAquaria recommends 130 gallons (492 L); MarineCollectors recommends 125 gallons (473 L); given the species' active swimming behavior and adult size of up to 24 cm, 500 L is used as a round conservative minimum.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been documented reliably. The species is understood to be a pelagic broadcast spawner; adults are typically observed in pairs in the wild, and the species is reported to become sexually mature at around 2 years of age. Replication of spawning cues in a home aquarium is generally considered infeasible.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page for Magnificent Foxface (Siganus magnificus). Provided adult size (9 in / ~22.9 cm), minimum tank size (130 gal / 492 L), temperature (72–78°F / 22–25.6°C), pH (8.1–8.4), dKH (8–12), salinity (1.020–1.025), care level (moderate), diet (herbivore), temperament (peaceful), reef safe (with caution).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 24.0 cm SL), temperature range (24–28°C preferred; modeled preferred 28.7°C mean), native range (Eastern Indian Ocean: Thailand and Java), habitat (reef-associated, 2–30 m), taxonomy (Siganidae, Acanthuriformes), diet (algae and small invertebrates, trophic level 2.7). Temperature is wider than trade sources; averaged range 22–28°C used.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Magnificent rabbitfish
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (24 cm TL, rarely >20 cm), native range (Andaman Sea: Mergui Archipelago, Surin and Similan Islands), habitat (sheltered reefs among branching corals, 2–30 m), lifespan (10–12 years), sexual maturity (~2 years), taxonomy, and original description (Lo magnificus, Burgess 1977). Adults commonly occur in pairs; juveniles secretive in corals.

additional.name: MarineCollectors
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (9 in / ~22.9 cm), minimum tank size (125 gal / 473 L), temperature (72–78°F), pH (7.8–8.5; wider range than LiveAquaria — 8.1–8.4 retained as more conservative standard reef parameter), salinity (1.022–1.026). Averaged salinity range 1.020–1.026 used.

additional.name: Reef Chasers – Magnificent Foxface Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed 100+ gallon minimum recommendation, herbivorous diet (marine algae, nori, pellets), peaceful temperament with intraspecific aggression, reef-safe-with-caution status, venomous spines, and color-change behavior. Noted lid recommended due to occasional jumping tendency.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner; adults observed in pairs in the wild; no reliable captive breeding documented. Reported to reach sexual maturity at approximately 2 years.
